edge: Contrastive loss has been increasingly used in learning representations from multiple modalities.
The nature of the contrastive loss encourages modalities to exactly match each other in the latent space.
An open question is how the modality alignment affects the downstream task performance.
Exact modality alignment is sub-optimal in general for downstream prediction tasks.
The key of better performance lies in meaningful latent modality structures instead of perfect modality alignment.
Three general approaches are proposed to construct latent modality structures.
A deep feature separation loss is designed for intra-modality regularization.
A Brownian-bridge loss is designed for inter-modality regularization.
A geometric consistency loss is designed for both intra- and inter-modality regularization.
Extensive experiments are conducted on two popular multi-modal representation learning frameworks.
Experiments are tested on a variety of tasks including zero/few-shot image classification, image-text retrieval, visual question answering, visual reasoning, and visual entailment.
Our method achieves consistent improvements over existing methods, demonstrating the effectiveness and generalizability of our proposed approach.
The proposed approach demonstrates the effectiveness and generalizability of latent modality structure regularization.

edge: There is a question of whether bridging and aligning the two modalities always brings benefits to downstream tasks in multi-modal representation learning.
The modality gap is defined as the distance between the feature distributions of the two modalities, closely related in multi-modal representation learning.
Modality alignment can be considered as reducing the modality gap in multi-modal representation learning.

Contrastive loss is conjectured to reduce the modality gap by pulling positive image and text data together for better representation, although recent study shows evidence that contrastive learning does not always reduce the modality gap.
A reduced modality gap does not always guarantee better performance in downstream applications as shown in empirical analysis.
When the modality gap is zero, it results in the information gap between the two modalities in multi-modal representation learning.
Representations with zero modality gap can only preserve predictive information present in both of the modalities at the cost of losing the modality-specific information.

This paper investigates the latent modality structures within multi-modal representation learning.
The analysis and examination of the modality gap in the latent feature space reveal insights into reducing the modality gap.
Meaningful latent features structures are advocated to benefit downstream applications.
Three regularization methods are designed to construct meaningful latent structures, including deep feature separation loss, brownian bridge loss, and geometric consistency loss.
Extensive experiments on vision-language tasks confirm the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed approach.
